edge_cases: Queries where the intermediate ARRAY result from bitmap_to_array is required for other computations
Projects containing multiple bitmap_to_array calls
Unnest operations applied to non-bitmap sources
Queries where session variable optimization is disabled
framework_notes: Matches LogicalTableFunction over LogicalProject pattern in the Memo. Modifies the Project operator's column reference map in-place and replaces the TableFunction operator. Executed during logical optimization phase as an RBO rule.

summary: This rule optimizes Logical Intersect operations by conditionally inserting Distinct (Aggregation) operators on input children. It uses statistics to verify if deduplicating inputs significantly reduces row count before applying the transformation.
ra_condition: Estimated cardinality of δ(R) is significantly lower than R (and similarly for S)
correctness_conditions: The Intersect operator must implement INTERSECT DISTINCT semantics
Adding Distinct to inputs must not alter the set of unique rows returned
Session variable isCboEnableIntersectAddDistinct must be enabled
performance_impact: Beneficial when inputs have high duplicate rates, reducing data volume for shuffle and sort during Intersect execution. May hurt performance if inputs are already unique or statistics are inaccurate, adding unnecessary aggregation overhead.
edge_cases: Inputs are scans on unique keys (already distinct)
Statistics estimation errors cause incorrect plan selection
INTERSECT ALL semantics (rule should not apply)
Low duplicate rate where aggregation cost exceeds shuffle savings
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_INTERSECT in StarRocks Cascades Memo. Checks session variable before transformation. Uses StatisticsCalculator to estimate cardinality reduction. Adds LogicalAggregationOperator (GLOBAL, group by all columns) to children if beneficial.

summary: Transforms a Logical Filter operator containing a large IN predicate into a Logical Semi Join or Anti Join against a generated constant table (Raw Values) to optimize execution.
ra_condition: Session enableLargeInPredicate=true, exactly one LargeInPredicate, no OR predicates
correctness_conditions: Session variable enableLargeInPredicate must be enabled
Filter predicate must contain exactly one LargeInPredicate
Filter predicate must not contain OR compound operators
Join type must match IN (Semi Join) or NOT IN (Null Aware Anti Join)
Data types between column and constant table must be compatible or castable
performance_impact: Improves performance for large IN lists by utilizing Join execution strategies (e.g., Hash Join, Broadcast) instead of linear predicate evaluation. May introduce overhead for small lists due to plan complexity and operator creation.
edge_cases: Queries containing OR predicates combined with LargeInPredicate
Queries containing multiple LargeInPredicates in the same filter
Type mismatches requiring implicit casting between column and constant values
NOT IN predicates requiring null-aware anti-join semantics
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_FILTER pattern. Transforms to LOGICAL_JOIN + LOGICAL_RAW_VALUES. Check method throws exceptions for unsupported cases (OR, multiple INs) rather than returning false. Relies on downstream physical rules for join implementation and property enforcement.

summary: Transforms a Logical Filter containing a large IN predicate into a Semi/Anti Join with a Raw Values table to optimize execution of large constant lists.
ra_condition: enableLargeInPredicate=true, single LargeInPredicate, no OR predicates
correctness_conditions: IN list values are treated as a constant relation V
Join type matches IN (Semi) or NOT IN (Null-Aware Anti) semantics
Type casting is applied if join key types mismatch
Remaining predicates are preserved on the left input
performance_impact: Beneficial for very large IN lists by leveraging hash join instead of linear predicate evaluation; may add overhead for small lists due to join planning complexity.
edge_cases: Queries with OR predicates (unsupported)
Multiple large IN predicates in one query (unsupported)
Type mismatches between column and IN list values
NOT IN queries requiring NULL semantics handling
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_FILTER pattern. Generates LogicalJoin and LogicalRawValues. Throws exception on unsupported patterns (OR, multiple large INs). Join hint is set to BROADCAST.

summary: Reorders a Left Semi/Anti Join above an Inner Join by pushing the Semi/Anti Join down to filter the left input earlier, reducing intermediate cardinality before the Inner Join.
ra_condition: Top join is LEFT_SEMI or LEFT_ANTI, Bottom join is INNER, Top predicate references only R and T columns
correctness_conditions: Top join type must be LEFT_SEMI_JOIN or LEFT_ANTI_JOIN
Bottom join type must not be OUTER JOIN
Bottom join must not have a LIMIT
No explicit JOIN HINTS on either join
Top join predicate columns must be subset of (BottomJoin.LeftOutput ∪ TopJoin.RightOutput)
performance_impact: Beneficial when T filters R significantly, reducing rows before joining S. May hurt if physical distribution requirements change (e.g. shuffle vs broadcast) or if S is very small.
edge_cases: Complex projections requiring column mapping rewriting
Queries where S is small enough for broadcast join making original order faster
Predicates referencing columns from S (rule will not trigger)
framework_notes: Cascades RBO rule matching LogicalJoin patterns. Operates on GroupExpressions in Memo. Does not trigger physical Enforcers directly but affects logical plan shape for later physical optimization.

summary: Reorders a Left Semi or Left Anti join positioned above an Inner or Left join by pushing the semi/anti join down to operate on the left-most input first, reducing cardinality early provided the semi-join predicate does not reference columns from the intermediate join's right input.
ra_condition: Predicate of ⋉ references only columns from R and T
correctness_conditions: Top join must be LEFT_SEMI_JOIN or LEFT_ANTI_JOIN
Bottom join must not be an Outer Join
Bottom join must not have a Limit
No Join Hints specified on either join
Semi/Anti join predicate columns must be subset of (Output(R) + Output(T))
performance_impact: Beneficial when filtering R with T significantly reduces cardinality before joining with S, leading to smaller hash tables or probe sides. May hurt performance if the cost of evaluating the semi-join early outweighs the benefit or if join algorithms selected for the new plan are less efficient.
edge_cases: Queries with explicit join hints forcing order
Bottom join is an Outer Join (forbidden by rule)
Bottom join has a Limit clause (forbidden by rule)
Semi-join predicate references columns from S (middle table)
framework_notes: Cascades RBO rule operating on LogicalJoin patterns within the Memo. It does not trigger physical Enforcer insertion as it transforms logical to logical. It expands the search space by adding equivalent logical expressions to the Memo groups for the Cost-Based Optimizer to evaluate.

summary: Pushes filter predicates and limit constraints from CTE consumers into the CTE producer operator to reduce the amount of data materialized or computed within the CTE definition.
ra_condition: All consumers of the CTE have registered predicates or limits in the CTEContext, and limit pushdown only occurs if no predicates are registered.
correctness_conditions: The CTE must be referenced by the exact number of consumers recorded in CTEContext.
For predicates, the disjunction (OR) of all consumer predicates must cover all rows required by any consumer.
For limits, the maximum limit value among consumers must be used to satisfy all consumers.
Limit pushdown is skipped if predicate pushdown is applicable to avoid interaction complexity.
performance_impact: Beneficial when CTE computation is expensive and predicates are selective, reducing I/O and CPU. May have limited benefit if the OR-combined predicate is not selective or if limit semantics depend on ordering not preserved by the global limit.
edge_cases: CTEs where consumers require different sort orders for LIMIT (global limit without order may be incorrect).
Mixed constraints where not all consumers share the same type of constraint (limit vs predicate).
CTEs with only one consumer (effectively subquery optimization).
Non-deterministic predicates pushed into CTE.
framework_notes: Matches LogicalCTEProduceOperator pattern. Relies on CTEContext populated during optimization to track consumer constraints. Predicates are processed by ScalarRangePredicateExtractor to simplify range conditions. Does not directly trigger Enforcer insertion but alters logical plan structure for downstream physical optimization.

summary: Pushes down filters (combined as OR) or limits (taken as MAX) from CTE consumers into the CTE producer definition to reduce data processing early in the query plan.
ra_condition: All consumers registered predicates OR (all registered limits AND no predicates registered)
correctness_conditions: CTE must have at least one consumer (consumeNums > 0)
For predicate pushdown: Every consumer must have contributed a predicate to the CTEContext
For limit pushdown: Every consumer must have contributed a limit and no predicates are present in CTEContext
Combined predicate is the logical OR of all consumer predicates (row needed if it satisfies any consumer)
Pushed limit is the maximum of all consumer limits (producing more than max is useless for all)
performance_impact: Beneficial when CTE definition is expensive (scans/joins) as it reduces input cardinality early. May hurt if OR predicate is complex or prevents index usage. Limit pushdown is restricted when filters exist, potentially missing combined optimization opportunities.
edge_cases: CTE referenced by only a subset of consumers with constraints (rule won't fire)
Mixed constraints (some consumers have limits, some have filters) prevents limit pushdown
Recursive CTEs may have different semantics not covered by this rule
Predicates that are not safe to evaluate before CTE materialization
framework_notes: Matches LogicalCTEProduceOperator in Cascades memo. Relies on CTEContext populated by CTEConsume operators during optimization. Operates in Logical phase. Does not enforce physical properties. Limit pushdown is mutually exclusive with predicate pushdown in this implementation.

summary: This rule merges two consecutive Logical Aggregation operators (separated by a column-pruning Project) into a single Aggregation operator, reducing computation stages when aggregate functions are compatible and grouping keys are compatible.
ra_condition: G1 ⊇ G2, π is identity projection, agg functions in ALLOW_MERGE_AGGREGATE_FUNCTIONS, no DISTINCT, no predicate/limit on lower agg
correctness_conditions: Project operator must be identity mapping (column pruning only, no expressions)
Lower aggregation must have no predicate or limit
Lower aggregation grouping keys must be superset of upper aggregation grouping keys
All aggregate functions must be in allowed mergeable set (SUM, MAX, MIN, BITMAP_UNION, HLL_UNION, PERCENTILE_UNION, ANY_VALUE)
No DISTINCT aggregates allowed
Upper aggregation functions must match lower aggregation functions on same columns (except MAX/MIN/ANY_VALUE on grouping columns)
performance_impact: Beneficial: Reduces aggregation stages from 2 to 1, eliminating intermediate materialization and shuffle overhead. Potentially harmful: None significant, as merge only occurs when semantically equivalent.
edge_cases: DISTINCT aggregates cannot be merged
Unsupported aggregate functions (AVG, COUNT, etc.) block merging
Lower aggregation with predicate or limit prevents merge
Grouping key mismatch (upper has keys not in lower) blocks merge
Project with expressions (not column pruning) prevents merge
framework_notes: This is an RBO rule in Cascades framework. Matches pattern LOGICAL_AGGR -> LOGICAL_PROJECT -> LOGICAL_AGGR. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion as it operates on logical plan. Reduces Memo group expression count by collapsing two aggregation operators into one, potentially reducing explore depth for downstream rules.

summary: This rule transposes a Filter and Project operator within a correlated Apply subquery where the Project feeds an Aggregation. It modifies the Project to output columns required by the Filter, allowing the Filter to be positioned directly below the Aggregation operator.
ra_condition: Subquery is correlated; Aggregation has no grouping keys OR Apply is Existential; Filter columns (excluding correlation) are added to Project output map
correctness_conditions: Project operator must be modified to pass through all non-correlated columns referenced by the Filter predicate
Filter predicate semantics must remain equivalent when evaluated on Project output columns versus original input columns
Correlation columns referenced in Filter are resolved by the outer Apply context and excluded from Project mapping adjustments
Transformation is restricted to Scalar subqueries (empty grouping) or Existential subqueries to ensure aggregation semantics are preserved
performance_impact: Beneficial for normalizing the logical plan to enable downstream optimizations like Aggregation-Filter fusion or Project elimination. Minimal overhead as it primarily adjusts column mappings. May prevent early filtering if Project contains expensive computations, but rule preserves existing project expressions.
edge_cases: Aggregations with grouping keys are explicitly excluded by the check method
Non-correlated subqueries do not trigger this rule
Filter predicates referencing columns not originally in Project output are handled by extending the Project map
Complex scalar expressions in Project are preserved in the new Project map
framework_notes: Matches specific LogicalApply-LogicalAggr-LogicalProject-LogicalFilter pattern in StarRocks Cascades Memo. RBO rule that transforms logical operators without enforcing physical properties. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion. Controls optimization depth by simplifying the operator tree structure under Apply.

summary: This rule prunes unused output columns from a Logical Window Operator based on the columns required by parent operators, reducing unnecessary computation.
ra_condition: W' = \{ w \in W \mid \text{output}(w) \in C_{req} \} \cup \text{internal\_deps}
correctness_conditions: All columns required by parent operators are preserved in the new window output
Columns referenced within retained window functions (partition, order, aggregate) are marked as required from child
Window functions are deterministic and have no side effects
performance_impact: Beneficial by reducing CPU and memory usage for unused window calculations. Negligible overhead when all columns are required.
edge_cases: No window columns required by parent (delegates to PruneEmptyWindowRule)
All window columns required (rule returns no transformation)
Complex expressions within window functions require accurate dependency tracking
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_WINDOW pattern. Relies on LogicalWindowOperator.deriveRequiredColumns to propagate input requirements to children. Does not insert Enforcers directly.

summary: Pushes a LogicalProject operator below a LogicalCTEAnchor to the anchor's right child, enabling column pruning within the CTE reference structure.
ra_condition: cols ⊆ output(R) AND CTEAnchor semantics allow projection pushdown
correctness_conditions: All columns in the projection list must be produced by the right child of the CTE Anchor
The CTE Anchor must not introduce dependencies on columns excluded by the projection
Semantic equivalence of the CTE definition and reference must be maintained
performance_impact: Beneficial for column pruning, reducing memory usage and IO for CTE scans. May hurt if it prevents CTE plan sharing across different references with different projections.
edge_cases: Recursive CTEs where projection affects recursion schema
Projects containing complex expressions that should remain shared
CTE Anchors where the right child does not supply all projected columns
framework_notes: Matches LogicalProject on top of LogicalCTEAnchor in the Memo. Creates new LogicalPlan expressions. Does not trigger physical Enforcer insertion directly as it is a logical transformation. Explores deeper into CTE anchor children.

summary: This rule ensures that all output columns of a Logical Except operator are marked as required from its children, preventing column pruning from removing columns necessary for set-based row comparison semantics.
ra_condition: R' and S' must produce full schemas of R and S respectively
correctness_conditions: Except operation requires full row equality comparison
Child operators must match the Except operator's output schema
Pruning columns before Except would alter set semantics
performance_impact: Prevents performance optimization via column pruning on Except inputs; ensures correctness at the cost of potentially processing unnecessary columns in children.
edge_cases: Except All vs Except Distinct (both require full row comparison)
Queries where parent only selects a subset of Except output columns
framework_notes: Modifies OptimizerContext TaskContext required columns directly; returns empty list to indicate no structural transformation, relying on context side-effects to guide child exploration.

summary: Pushes a TopN/Limit operator below a Rank-related Window operator to reduce data processing volume per partition, converting global TopN to PartitionTopN where applicable to optimize pipeline execution.
ra_condition: ω is rank-related, λ orders by ω's output ascending, pipeline engine enabled
correctness_conditions: TopN order by column must match the window function's output column
TopN order direction must be ascending
Window function must be rank-related (e.g., row_number, rank, dense_rank)
Pipeline execution engine must be enabled
Sort properties between consecutive window operators must be compatible to avoid extra sort enforcement
performance_impact: Beneficial when partitioning significantly reduces data volume before expensive window computation; potentially negative if extra sort enforcement is required between stacked window operators due to mismatched sort columns.
edge_cases: Non-pipeline execution engine
Window function is not rank-related
TopN order is descending
TopN order column differs from window output
Multiple window operators with conflicting sort requirements
TopN operator has no limit defined
framework_notes: Matches LogicalTopN -> LogicalProject -> LogicalWindow pattern in Memo. Requires Pipeline Engine session variable. Transforms LogicalTopN into PartitionTopN operator. Explicitly checks EnforceSortColumns equality to prevent negative gain from Enforcer insertion in multi-window scenarios.

summary: This rule performs column pruning on supported scan operators by replacing the original scan with a new one that reads only the columns required by parent operators and local predicates, reducing I/O and memory usage.
ra_condition: C_req = (RequiredOutput ∩ C_all) ∪ Cols(Predicate) ∪ Cols(PartitionPredicate) AND C_req ⊂ C_all
correctness_conditions: The pruned column set must contain all columns referenced by parent operators in the plan.
The pruned column set must contain all columns referenced in the scan operator's filter predicate.
The pruned column set must contain all columns referenced in partition pruning predicates for OLAP scans.
If the resulting column set is empty, a single column must be selected to support COUNT(*) semantics if optimization is enabled.
performance_impact: Significantly reduces I/O and network traffic for wide tables by reading fewer columns. Improves memory efficiency during execution. Planning overhead is minimal.
edge_cases: SELECT * queries where all columns are required result in no transformation.
Scan operator types not listed in the SUPPORTED set are ignored.
COUNT(*) queries depend on the enable_count_star_optimization session variable.
Queries with predicates referencing columns not in the SELECT list must retain those predicate columns.
framework_notes: Matches LogicalScanOperator subtypes via MultiOpPattern. Relies on top-down propagation of RequiredColumns in TaskContext. Returns empty list if no columns can be pruned to avoid infinite loops. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion.

summary: This rule identifies queries calculating MIN or MAX aggregates over Iceberg tables and annotates the scan operator to enable file-level statistics optimization, allowing data skipping based on min/max values without changing the logical plan structure.
ra_condition: agg functions are MIN/MAX, predicates reference only partition columns, no limit clause, table is Iceberg
correctness_conditions: Session variable enable_min_max_optimization is true
Scan operator is LogicalIcebergScan
Scan operator has no limit (limit == -1)
Scan and Aggregation predicates reference only partition columns
Grouping keys (if any) are subset of partition columns
Table is unpartitioned or all partition columns are identity columns
Aggregation functions are strictly MIN or MAX
Aggregation arguments are single column references (no expressions)
performance_impact: Significantly improves performance for MIN/MAX queries on large Iceberg tables by skipping files or row groups using column statistics; negligible planning overhead as it only sets a flag.
edge_cases: Queries with LIMIT clause will not trigger optimization
Filters on non-partition columns disable the optimization
Aggregations like SUM, COUNT, or AVG are not supported
Expressions inside aggregation arguments (e.g., MIN(col + 1)) are not supported
Partitioned tables with non-identity partition columns may not qualify
framework_notes: Transform method returns empty list, indicating in-place modification of the ScanOperator's optimization flags rather than generating alternative plan expressions in the Memo; matches pattern Agg -> Project -> IcebergScan.

summary: This rule optimizes Logical Aggregation operators by removing redundant grouping keys found in the child Logical Project, such as duplicates, constants, or columns functionally dependent on other grouping keys, potentially eliminating the aggregation operator entirely if no grouping is needed.
ra_condition: G' ⊂ G where removed keys are duplicates, constants, or functionally dependent on retained keys via P
correctness_conditions: Removed grouping keys must be duplicates, constants, or functionally dependent on retained keys.
If all keys are constants and aggregations exist, at least one constant key must be retained to preserve empty-input semantics.
Having predicates must be rewritten to reference the new output columns if aggregation remains.
Grouping expressions containing non-deterministic functions cannot be pruned.
performance_impact: Reduces shuffle cardinality and hash table memory usage in distributed execution; eliminates aggregation operator overhead if grouping is purely constant without aggregations.
edge_cases: Empty tables with constant group by and aggregations require retaining one key to distinguish empty result from single row.
Total pruning of grouping keys is blocked if a HAVING predicate exists.
Grouping expressions containing non-deterministic functions prevent pruning.
All keys pruned without aggregations converts Aggregation to Limit 1.
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_AGGR on LOGICAL_PROJECT. RBO rule. Rewrites predicates (Having) and adds post-aggregation projections to maintain schema. May insert LogicalLimit if Aggregation is removed. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion.

summary: Transforms a scalar correlated subquery (represented as LogicalApply with Aggregation) into a Window Function operation to enable more efficient execution plans by avoiding nested-loop semantics.
ra_condition: Apply is scalar, aggregation functions are supported (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX), no DISTINCT, inner joins are cross joins
correctness_conditions: Apply operator must be scalar (returns single value per outer row)
Aggregation functions must be in whitelist (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX)
Aggregation functions cannot use DISTINCT
Only one Aggregation operator allowed in subquery subtree
All joins in outer and inner subtrees must be Cross Joins
No LIMIT operator in the subquery subtree
Correlation predicates must be rewritable for window partitioning
performance_impact: Significantly improves performance by eliminating expensive nested-loop Apply execution in favor of single-pass Window computation; may introduce sorting or shuffling overhead if partition keys require data movement
edge_cases: Subqueries with DISTINCT aggregations are not supported
Subqueries with unsupported aggregate functions (e.g., GROUP_CONCAT) are ignored
Complex correlation predicates not matching expected patterns prevent transformation
Subqueries containing multiple Aggregation operators are not transformed
Subqueries containing LIMIT clauses are not transformed
framework_notes: RBO transformation rule in Cascades framework; matches specific Memo group expressions (Filter-Project-Apply-Agg); creates LogicalWindowOperator which may trigger physical property enforcers (Sort/Exchange) during physical planning

summary: This rule is designed to optimize Logical Repeat operators by removing output columns that are not required by the parent operator, reducing data processing overhead.
ra_condition: required ⊂ Repeat.output_columns
correctness_conditions: The set of required columns must be a subset of the Repeat operator's original output columns.
Columns removed must not be referenced by any parent operators in the plan.
The Repeat operator's semantics (row generation) must remain unchanged for the retained columns.
performance_impact: Beneficial when the Repeat operator generates wide rows but downstream operators only consume a subset of columns, reducing memory usage and serialization cost. Negligible overhead for rule matching.
edge_cases: Queries where all columns produced by Repeat are required (no pruning occurs).
Repeat operators with side effects or dependencies on pruned columns (rare in logical plan).
Schemas where column references are ambiguous or dynamically resolved.
framework_notes: Matches OperatorType.LOGICAL_REPEAT in the Cascades Memo. The provided snippet returns an empty list, suggesting it may rely on context side-effects for column propagation or represents a no-op case when no pruning is applicable; typically, such rules return a new OptExpression with a modified operator.

summary: Merges a local LIMIT operator directly into supported child operators by setting the limit property on the child, eliminating the separate LIMIT node when no offset is present.
ra_condition: limit.isLocal() AND !limit.hasOffset() AND Op ∈ SUPPORT_OPERATOR
correctness_conditions: Limit must be local (applies to current result set without global ordering requirements)
Limit must not have an offset (OFFSET 0 only)
Child operator must support direct limit property setting
Semantic equivalence must be preserved (same rows returned in same order)
performance_impact: Beneficial: eliminates operator node overhead, enables early limit application in scans/operators, reduces data flow through pipeline and memory usage. No known negative cases for supported operators as it's a pure optimization.
edge_cases: LIMIT with OFFSET > 0 cannot be merged (requires separate handling)
Non-local limits (distributed/global limits requiring coordination)
Operators not in SUPPORT_OPERATOR set (e.g., JOINs, certain complex operators)
LIMIT requiring specific ordering that child cannot guarantee
framework_notes: RBO rule in StarRocks Cascades framework. Matches LOGICAL_LIMIT with supported child operators via Pattern.create. Does not trigger enforcer insertion. Reduces memo group expression count by eliminating LIMIT node and merging properties into child operator. Check method validates isLocal() before transform.

summary: Transforms a Logical OLAP Scan with a disjunctive (OR) predicate into a Union of multiple scans, allowing each branch to optimize predicate evaluation independently.
ra_condition: Predicate P is disjunctive, estimated benefit exceeds threshold, and union child count is within session limit
correctness_conditions: The union of split predicates must be logically equivalent to the original predicate
For non-IN predicates, negation of preceding branches is applied to ensure disjointness
Column references in predicates must exist in the scan output
performance_impact: Improves performance when OR predicates prevent index usage or partition pruning; may degrade performance if scan overhead exceeds pruning benefits or selectivity is high
edge_cases: External tables are not supported
Predicates with unknown selectivity may prevent rewrite
Limited by session variable ScanOrToUnionLimit
High selectivity predicates (already efficient) are skipped
framework_notes: Matches LogicalOlapScanOperator. Uses StatisticsCalcUtils for cost estimation. Checks session variables for thresholds. Implements disjointness logic for non-IN ORs via predicate negation.

summary: This rule acts as a guard mechanism rather than an optimization transformation. It matches Logical Apply operators (typically representing correlated subqueries) and immediately throws an UnsupportedException, explicitly declaring that this operation is not supported in the current optimizer phase.
ra_condition: Operator type is LOGICAL_APPLY
correctness_conditions: This is not a semantic transformation but a feature limitation enforcement
Any query plan matching this pattern is considered invalid for execution
performance_impact: No performance benefit; it halts query compilation to prevent execution of unimplemented features, avoiding potential runtime errors or incorrect results.
edge_cases: Queries containing correlated subqueries that decorrelate to Logical Apply
Lateral joins that are planned as Logical Apply
Complex nested subqueries requiring Apply semantics
framework_notes: Matches group expressions with LOGICAL_APPLY root. Throws exception immediately upon transformation attempt, preventing Memo exploration, Enforcer insertion, or further rule application for this plan branch.

summary: This rule pushes null-rejecting predicates that reference repeat columns from a LogicalFilter down through a LogicalRepeat operator. It creates a new Filter below the Repeat for pushable predicates while merging all original filter predicates with the Repeat's existing predicate.
ra_condition: p' ⊆ p where predicates in p' reference repeat columns and evaluate to FALSE when repeat columns are replaced with NULL
correctness_conditions: Predicates must reference at least one column defined by the Repeat operator
Predicates must be null-rejecting on repeat columns (evaluating to FALSE if repeat column is NULL)
Predicates must be deterministic and side-effect free (Utils.canPushDownPredicate check)
performance_impact: Beneficial when it reduces the cardinality of data entering the Repeat operator early, especially for expensive Repeat operations like Union or recursion. May introduce minor overhead due to redundant predicate evaluation in both the new Filter and the Repeat operator.
edge_cases: Predicates like 'col IS NULL' are not pushed because they evaluate to TRUE when col is NULL
Predicates not referencing any repeat columns are not pushed by this specific rule
Complex expressions involving repeat columns that do not constant-fold to FALSE when nullified
framework_notes: RBO rule in StarRocks Cascades optimizer. Matches GroupExpressions with LogicalFilter on top of LogicalRepeat. It modifies the Memo by replacing the matched expression with a transformed tree. It does not trigger Physical Enforcer insertion as it is a logical transformation.

summary: This rule targets Logical Join operators to optimize column usage by propagating required column sets to child operators. It updates the optimizer's TaskContext with the union of parent-required columns and join-predicate columns, enabling downstream column pruning without altering the plan structure directly in this transformation step.
ra_condition: req_L and req_R are minimal subsets of R and S columns needed to satisfy join condition and produce req
correctness_conditions: Join predicate columns must be included in req_L and req_R
Columns required by parent operator (req) must be producible from pruned inputs
Join type semantics (e.g., NULLs in Outer Joins) must not be affected by pruning non-referenced columns
Side effects on TaskContext must not violate Cascades memoization assumptions
performance_impact: Beneficial: Reduces memory usage, serialization overhead, and network shuffle size by eliminating unused columns early. Neutral: Minimal CPU overhead for set calculations; no structural plan changes generated by this specific transform method.
edge_cases: SELECT * queries where all columns are required
Joins where all columns are referenced in join predicates or filters
Queries where downstream operators require full row schemas
Scenarios where TaskContext side-effects are reset or ignored during Memo exploration
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_JOIN with two PATTERN_LEAF children. The transform method returns Collections.emptyList(), indicating it relies on TaskContext side-effects for column requirement propagation rather than generating new OptExpression alternatives in the Memo. Does not explicitly trigger Enforcer insertion.

summary: Pushes global aggregation operators (COUNT, MAX, MIN, DICT_MERGE) without grouping keys down to the LogicalMetaScan operator, rewriting them to compute results by aggregating pre-computed statistical metadata columns (e.g., row counts, min/max values) instead of scanning base data.
ra_condition: GroupKeys = ∅ ∧ AggFunc ∈ {COUNT, MAX, MIN, DICT_MERGE} ∧ Proj ≈ Identity ∨ (Constant ∧ HasCount) ∧ MetaScan.AggCols = ∅
correctness_conditions: Aggregation must be global (no GROUP BY keys).
Aggregation functions must be limited to COUNT, MAX, MIN, or DICT_MERGE.
Project operator must preserve column identity or use constants (for COUNT).
Metadata statistics (row counts, min, max) must be accurate and cover the entire scan range.
No filtering predicates exist between Aggregation and MetaScan (implied by pattern).
performance_impact: Significantly improves performance for global aggregate queries by avoiding full table scans, reading only lightweight metadata statistics instead. Negligible downside unless metadata is stale or locking overhead on stats is high.
edge_cases: Queries with WHERE clauses (predicates) cannot use this as stats apply to whole partitions/blocks.
Aggregations with GROUP BY are excluded.
Unsupported aggregate functions (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT DISTINCT).
MetaScan operators that already have aggregation columns defined.
framework_notes: Matches Cascades pattern LOGICAL_AGGR -> LOGICAL_PROJECT -> LOGICAL_META_SCAN. Modifies the LogicalMetaScan operator's internal mapping to expose statistical columns. Does not change the logical tree height but changes the leaf operator's semantics to read metadata. RBO rule executed during logical optimization phase. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion.

summary: Detects data skew in join operations using statistics (MCV/histogram) or explicit hints, and optimizes by adding salt columns to redistribute skewed values across shuffle partitions for better parallelism
ra_output_pattern: R' ⋈_(q ∧ salt) S' (with salt columns added to both join inputs)
ra_condition: Join is INNER or LEFT OUTER, has equality predicates on columns, statistics show skew (MCV ratio > threshold) or HINT_SKEW is set
correctness_conditions: Salt column addition must preserve join semantics and result cardinality
Skewed values must be correctly identified from MCV statistics or hint
Both join sides must receive compatible salt distribution logic
Original join predicate must be preserved alongside salt-based redistribution
Null handling must remain consistent with original join type
performance_impact: Beneficial when join keys have significant data skew (MCV rows exceed threshold percentage). Reduces shuffle skew and improves parallelism. Adds overhead when no skew exists due to extra projection, salt computation, and additional shuffle operations
edge_cases: No statistics or histogram available for join columns
RIGHT OUTER JOIN or FULL OUTER JOIN not supported by this rule
Non-column equality predicates (e.g., expressions in join condition)
Small tables where skew optimization overhead exceeds benefit
Join hint SKEW specified but skew column cannot be found
Multiple equality predicates where only one column is skewed
framework_notes: RBO rule in Cascades framework. Matches LogicalJoin with PATTERN_LEAF children. Triggers PushDownJoinOnExpressionToChildProject as successor rule for predicate pushdown. May require physical Enforcer for shuffle distribution strategy. Uses OptimizerContext SessionVariable for threshold configuration

summary: This rule rewrites a logical scan on an Iceberg 'partitions' metadata table by injecting a global aggregation operator on top of the scan. It groups by 'partition_value' and applies specific aggregate functions (SUM, MAX, ANY_VALUE) to metric columns to ensure partition-level statistics are returned instead of file-level details.
ra_condition: T.metadata_type = PARTITIONS ∧ ¬Scan.transformed
correctness_conditions: The underlying Iceberg metadata scan returns granular data (e.g., per data file) that requires aggregation to represent partition-level semantics.
The 'partition_value' column uniquely identifies a partition for grouping purposes.
All columns in the scan output are either grouping keys or have defined aggregate functions in the rule logic.
The scan operator has not been previously transformed by this rule to ensure idempotency.
performance_impact: Beneficial when the raw metadata scan returns multiple rows per partition (e.g., one per file), reducing row count for downstream operators. Overhead is negligible due to the small size of metadata tables. May be unnecessary if the underlying scan implementation changes to return pre-aggregated data.
edge_cases: Queries selecting columns not handled in the switch statement will throw a StarRocksConnectorException.
If the Iceberg specification adds new metadata columns, this rule must be updated to avoid failures.
Predicates applied before this rule are preserved in the new scan operator, ensuring filter pushdown compatibility.
framework_notes: In the Cascades framework, this rule matches a single LogicalIcebergMetadataScanOperator group expression. It produces a new group expression with a LogicalAggregationOperator as the head and a marked-as-transformed LogicalIcebergMetadataScanOperator as the child. The 'isTransformed' flag prevents infinite recursion or re-application of the rule on the new scan node. It does not explicitly trigger Enforcer insertion but adds a logical operator layer.

summary: This rule performs join reassociation, transforming a left-deep join tree into a right-deep or bushy tree (e.g., (A ⋈ B) ⋈ C → A ⋈ (B ⋈ C)) to expand the search space for join ordering.
ra_condition: Join types support associativity (typically INNER JOIN or CROSS JOIN)
correctness_conditions: Join associativity must hold for the specific join types involved
Join predicates and filter predicates must be correctly split and redistributed to maintain semantic equivalence
Output column schema and null semantics must be preserved across the transformation
Transform mask must be updated to prevent infinite transformation cycles
performance_impact: Beneficial for finding optimal bushy join plans that reduce intermediate result sizes; however, it increases optimization time due to a larger search space and may generate suboptimal plans if cost estimation is inaccurate.
edge_cases: Outer joins require strict null-rejection property checks before association
Queries with a large number of joins may suffer from optimization timeout due to combinatorial explosion
Predicates referencing columns from all three tables require careful splitting logic
framework_notes: Logical equivalence transformation rule in Cascades framework; matches GroupExpressions with Join-on-Join structure; uses TransformMask to avoid cycles; does not enforce physical properties directly but enables subsequent physical rules.

summary: This rule separates predicates embedded in a Logical Scan Operator into those that can be pushed down to the storage engine and those that must be evaluated by the compute engine. It transforms a Scan with mixed predicates into a Filter operator on top of a Scan with only pushable predicates, ensuring correct column projections and limit handling.
ra_condition: P_reserved is not empty AND P_pushed is not equal to P
correctness_conditions: P_pushed AND P_reserved must be logically equivalent to original P
All columns referenced in P_reserved must be included in the output columns of the new Scan operator
If a Limit exists on the Scan, it must be moved to the Filter operator to ensure rows are filtered before limiting
Session variables 'enable_predicate_expr_reuse' and 'enable_scan_predicate_expr_reuse' must be enabled
performance_impact: Beneficial by allowing storage engines to execute supported predicates efficiently (pushdown) while handling complex expressions in the compute layer. Prevents scan execution errors due to unsupported predicates. Minor overhead from adding an explicit Filter operator.
edge_cases: Queries with LIMIT clauses require the limit to be moved above the filter to maintain correctness
Scans with existing projections must be merged with columns required by reserved predicates
Subfield pruning expressions must be explicitly added to scan projections to ensure correct column access paths
framework_notes: Matches LogicalScanOperator patterns in Cascades. Controlled by session variables 'enable_predicate_expr_reuse' and 'enable_scan_predicate_expr_reuse'. Generates a new OptExpression group with Filter->Scan structure.

summary: This rule pushes down conjuncts from a Logical Join's ON clause to its child operators when the predicates reference columns from only one side, enabling earlier filtering and reducing join input size.
ra_condition: θR references only R, θS references only S, Join type supports pushdown (e.g., INNER JOIN), and predicate pushdown is enabled in context
correctness_conditions: Predicates pushed to a child must reference only columns from that child's schema
Join type must preserve semantics (e.g., cannot push non-null-generating predicates to the null-supplying side of an OUTER JOIN)
Predicate evaluation must be deterministic and side-effect free
Null semantics must be preserved when pushing to outer join sides
performance_impact: Beneficial by reducing row count early in the plan, reducing network shuffle volume and join computation cost. Minimal risk of performance degradation unless predicate evaluation is significantly more expensive than join processing.
edge_cases: Outer Joins where pushing predicates to the null-producing side changes semantics (turns Outer to Inner)
Predicates referencing columns from both join sides cannot be pushed down
Join operators already marked with hasPushDownJoinOnClause to prevent infinite loops
Queries where join predicate is null or empty trigger check failure
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_JOIN with PATTERN_LEAF children in the Cascades Memo. Uses a boolean flag on the operator to prevent re-application. Transforms Logical to Logical without triggering Physical Enforcers. Returns empty list if transformation yields no structural change.

summary: Combines two adjacent Logical Project operators into one by substituting column references from the upper project with expressions from the lower project, while preserving limits and side effects.
ra_condition: Two consecutive Logical Project operators exist in the plan tree
correctness_conditions: All column references in the upper project must map to valid expressions in the lower project
Side-effecting functions (specifically ASSERT_TRUE) in the lower project must be retained in the merged output
The merged limit must be the strictest (minimum) of the two original limits, treating -1 as unlimited
performance_impact: Beneficial: reduces CPU overhead of multiple projection layers and simplifies the plan for downstream optimizations; Neutral/Negative: negligible, potentially slightly larger expressions but fewer operator nodes
edge_cases: Lower project contains ASSERT_TRUE calls that are not referenced by the upper project but must be executed
Handling of LIMIT -1 (unlimited) versus positive integer limits during merge
Constant folding opportunities triggered after expression substitution
framework_notes: Implements IRule in StarRocks Cascades; matches Pattern(Project -> Project); modifies Memo by replacing the group expression with a single Project OptExpression; does not enforce physical properties

summary: Rewrites COUNT(DISTINCT hll_column) to HLL_UNION_AGG(hll_column) in Logical Aggregation to enable efficient HLL state merging instead of raw value distinct counting.
ra_condition: ∃ f ∈ F : f = COUNT(DISTINCT h) ∧ type(h) = HLL, F' replaces f with HLL_UNION_AGG(h)
correctness_conditions: Column must be of HLL type
Aggregation must be COUNT with DISTINCT modifier
Aggregation must have exactly one child argument
System must handle the type difference between COUNT (BIGINT) and HLL_UNION_AGG (HLL) in subsequent phases or execution
performance_impact: Beneficial: Reduces shuffle data volume by merging HLL states instead of raw values. Lowers CPU cost for distinct calculation. Detrimental: None significant, adds minor function lookup overhead.
edge_cases: COUNT(DISTINCT) on non-HLL types
COUNT without DISTINCT
COUNT with multiple arguments
Scenarios requiring exact distinct counts (HLL is approximate)
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_AGGR with leaf children. Forces AggType to GLOBAL regardless of input. Preserves original return type in CallOperator. RBO rule in Cascades framework.

summary: This rule optimizes logical aggregation operators by eliminating specific aggregate functions (MAX, MIN, ANY_VALUE, etc.) when their argument column is already part of the GROUP BY clause, replacing the aggregation calculation with a direct column reference via a projection.
ra_condition: f ∈ {MAX, MIN, ANY_VALUE, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE} ∧ C ⊆ G ∧ ¬distinct(f)
correctness_conditions: The aggregate function argument must be a simple column reference
The argument column must be included in the GROUP BY keys (functional dependency)
The aggregate function must not be DISTINCT
The session variable enable_eliminate_agg must be enabled
performance_impact: Beneficial: Reduces CPU overhead by removing unnecessary aggregate function computations during the aggregation phase. The added projection operator is computationally cheap.
edge_cases: DISTINCT aggregations are ignored even if logically redundant
Complex expressions as arguments (e.g., MAX(col + 1)) are not supported
Unsupported aggregate functions like SUM or COUNT are not eliminated
Columns not present in the GROUP BY clause cannot be eliminated
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_AGGR operator patterns in the Cascades memo. Transforms into a LogicalProject wrapping a simplified LogicalAggregation. Does not trigger physical property enforcers. Controlled by session variable.

summary: Eliminates join operators when one child is logically empty (LogicalValues with 0 rows), simplifying the plan to an empty result or the non-empty side with null padding based on join type semantics.
ra_output_pattern: ∅ (Inner/Cross/Semi) OR R/S with nulls (Outer) OR R/S (Anti)
ra_condition: R = ∅ ∨ S = ∅ (detected via LogicalValues with empty rows)
correctness_conditions: Empty input must be strictly zero rows (LogicalValues)
Outer join null-padding semantics must be preserved for non-empty side
Join predicates must be applied to the resulting plan if they exist
Column references must be correctly mapped to output schema
performance_impact: Significant performance gain by avoiding join execution when one side is empty; reduces plan complexity and runtime cost. No known negative performance impact.
edge_cases: LogicalValues wrapped in LogicalProject (handled by skipping projects in check)
Join predicates present on the original join operator (preserved via Filter)
Full Outer Joins (preserves non-empty side with nulls)
Anti Joins (returns specific side based on emptiness)
Join operator must not have attached projection (Precondition in transform)
framework_notes: RBO rule (TF_PRUNE_EMPTY_JOIN). Matches LOGICAL_JOIN with PATTERN_LEAF children. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion. Reduces Memo group expression complexity by replacing Join with Values or Project. Requires Join operator projection to be null.

summary: This rule inlines a Common Table Expression (CTE) by replacing the LogicalCTEConsumeOperator with its underlying definition plan, provided the optimizer context determines that inlining is beneficial (e.g., single reference).
ra_condition: context.needInline(CTE_{id}) == true
correctness_conditions: The CTE definition plan must be attached as inputs to the Consume operator
The CTE must not be referenced multiple times where materialization is required for correctness or performance
The CTE definition must be side-effect free to allow safe duplication if referenced elsewhere
performance_impact: Beneficial when a CTE is referenced only once, eliminating materialization overhead and enabling further optimizations like predicate pushdown into the CTE body. Potentially harmful if a multi-reference CTE is inlined, causing redundant computation.
edge_cases: Recursive CTEs which cannot be simply inlined
CTEs referenced multiple times in the query plan
CTEs containing volatile functions where re-evaluation yields different results
Very large CTE definitions that increase plan complexity excessively
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_CTE_CONSUME operator within the Cascades memo. Relies on CteContext.needInline() for guard logic. Transforms by returning input.getInputs(), effectively removing the Consume node. Does not directly trigger physical enforcers but alters the logical tree for subsequent physical optimization passes.

summary: Pushes filter predicates down through a LogicalTableFunction operator to reduce input data volume, provided predicates only reference columns available before the function execution.
ra_condition: p_pushed references only columns from E (TVF outer refs)
correctness_conditions: Predicates must not reference columns produced by the Table Function
Predicates must be safe to push down (e.g., deterministic, no side effects)
Table Function must preserve row semantics equivalent to filtering before execution
performance_impact: Beneficial when Table Functions are computationally expensive; reduces rows processed by the function. Negligible negative impact.
edge_cases: Predicates referencing Table Function output columns cannot be pushed
Predicates containing volatile functions (e.g., now(), random()) may be blocked by Utils.canPushDownPredicate
Queries where no predicates match outer columns result in no transformation
framework_notes: Cascades RBO rule matching LogicalFilter over LogicalTableFunction. Returns empty list if no predicates pushed to avoid infinite re-matching. Constructs new OptExpression tree with split predicates.

summary: This rule optimizes query plans by detecting operators fed by an empty LogicalValues relation and replacing the entire subtree with a new empty LogicalValues operator, effectively short-circuiting execution for empty inputs.
ra_condition: Child = ∅ AND Op ∉ {γ_∅ (Global Aggregation), CTE_PRODUCE, ASSERT_ONE_ROW}
correctness_conditions: The parent operator must semantically return an empty result set when its input is empty
Global aggregations without grouping keys are excluded because they return one row even on empty input
The new empty relation must preserve the output schema (column types and names) of the original parent operator
performance_impact: Significant benefit by eliminating unnecessary computation steps when data is known to be empty at compile time; no known negative performance cases.
edge_cases: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM empty_table (Global Aggregation returns 1 row, not 0)
Queries involving CTE production logic
Queries with ASSERT_ONE_ROW operators expecting specific cardinality
framework_notes: Matches OptExpressions in the Memo where the child is a constant empty LogicalValues. As an RBO rule, it simplifies the logical plan before physical planning. It produces a LogicalValues leaf, so no Enforcer insertion is triggered for this specific transformation.

summary: Optimizes logical aggregation operators containing multiple DISTINCT aggregate functions. It determines whether to rewrite the plan using a Common Table Expression (CTE) strategy or a multi-function multi-stage strategy based on distinct column overlap, data types, and cost estimates.
ra_input_pattern: γ_{G, F}(E) where F contains ≥2 DISTINCT aggregates OR DISTINCT aggregate on complex constant
ra_output_pattern: γ_{G, F'}(ρ_{CTE}(γ_{G, F_partial}(E))) OR γ_{G, F'}(γ_{G, F_partial}(E))
ra_condition: Aggregation has multiple distinct functions with different distinct columns, or complex types prevent standard two-phase aggregation
correctness_conditions: Semantic equivalence of COUNT DISTINCT must be preserved across shuffle boundaries
Complex constant distinct counts are safely replaced by ANY_VALUE logic as per engine limitations
CTE reuse session variable must be enabled if CTE rewrite is selected for multi-column distincts
performance_impact: Beneficial when distinct columns differ across functions, reducing data shuffle volume compared to separate aggregations. May introduce CTE materialization overhead for simple cases where direct multi-stage aggregation suffices.
edge_cases: COUNT DISTINCT on complex types (Array, Struct, Map)
Session variable cbo_cte_reuse is disabled
Single tablet table property detected
GROUP_CONCAT or ARRAY_AGG on Decimal types
Aggregation operator contains a Limit clause
framework_notes: RBO rule (TF_REWRITE_MULTI_DISTINCT) matching LOGICAL_AGGR. Selects between MultiDistinctByCTERewriter and MultiDistinctByMultiFuncRewriter. Interacts with OptimizerContext for session variables and statistics.

summary: This rule optimizes Logical Intersect operators by pruning unused output columns. It propagates column requirements from the parent context down to the Intersect's children, ensuring only necessary columns are processed and passed up, reducing data volume.
ra_condition: Parent operator requires a subset S of the Intersect's output columns
correctness_conditions: All children must be projected to the exact same subset of columns S
Column order and data types in S must remain consistent across all children
Columns required by the parent must be preserved in S
Intersect semantics (duplicate elimination across inputs) must be maintained on the projected columns
performance_impact: Beneficial when intersecting wide tables where only a few columns are needed downstream, reducing I/O, memory, and network overhead. Minimal cost added by projection operators.
edge_cases: Queries where all Intersect output columns are required (no pruning occurs)
Intersect operations with complex expressions in children that cannot be easily projected
Schemas where child column types differ slightly requiring implicit casts before intersect
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_INTERSECT with PATTERN_MULTI_LEAF children. Updates OptimizerContext TaskContext required columns to push constraints. Returns emptyList indicating it may rely on context propagation or subsequent operator reconstruction to materialize the plan change rather than direct expression replacement.

summary: Pushes a TopN operator below a Union All operator, applying the limit and sort to each union branch individually while retaining a global TopN to merge results.
ra_condition: Union is UNION ALL, Order By columns ⊆ Union output, Limit ≤ threshold, Offset = 0
correctness_conditions: Union must be UNION ALL (no deduplication)
Order By columns must be present in Union output schema
TopN must not have an offset
TopN limit must be within configured threshold (CboPushDownTopNLimit)
Child branches must not have a stricter limit already
performance_impact: Beneficial when reducing row count early significantly lowers network/processing cost of Union and final sort. Potentially negative if sorting overhead per branch outweighs reduction benefits or if limit is large.
edge_cases: UNION DISTINCT
TopN with OFFSET
Order By columns not projected
Limit exceeds CboPushDownTopNLimit
Child has smaller limit
framework_notes: Matches LogicalTopN over LogicalUnion GroupExpressions. RBO rule that does not trigger Enforcer insertion. Creates new OptExpressions for pushed TopN in children groups. At least one child must be eligible for pushdown.

summary: This rule identifies aggregation queries computing MIN, MAX, or COUNT DISTINCT exclusively over partition columns and marks the scan operator to retrieve values from partition metadata instead of reading data files.
ra_condition: agg ∈ {MIN, MAX, COUNT DISTINCT}, cols ⊆ PartitionKeys(T), session_enabled=true
correctness_conditions: Aggregation functions must be strictly MIN, MAX, or COUNT DISTINCT
Scan operator output columns must be a subset of table partition columns
Session variable enable_partition_column_value_only_optimization must be enabled
Scan operator must not require non-partition columns (CanUseAnyColumn check)
performance_impact: Significant performance improvement by avoiding data file I/O and reading only partition metadata; no negative impact when conditions are met as it skips heavy I/O operations.
edge_cases: Aggregations like SUM, AVG, or COUNT (without DISTINCT) are not supported
Queries accessing non-partition columns in projection or aggregation will not trigger this rule
Currently optimized primarily for single partition column scenarios as per code comments
Materialized columns during prune stage might affect column check logic
framework_notes: Modifies LogicalScanOperator properties in-place (setUsePartitionColumnValueOnly) rather than generating new OptExpressions; matches LogicalAggr->LogicalProject->LogicalScan pattern; does not trigger Enforcer insertion; relies on side-effects on the input expression within the Memo.

summary: This rule pushes a local LIMIT operator without offset down through a UNION operator. It applies the limit to each branch of the union individually while retaining the limit constraint on the union operator itself to ensure global correctness.
ra_condition: offset = 0, limit is local (no global ordering or exchange required)
correctness_conditions: The limit must not have an offset (OFFSET 0)
The limit must be local (does not require global data exchange or sorting)
The final limit must be enforced on the Union operator after combining limited branches
performance_impact: Beneficial when union branches are expensive (e.g., scans, joins) as it reduces data volume early. May add minor overhead for managing additional limit operators per branch.
edge_cases: Limits with non-zero offsets
Limits requiring global ordering (Top-N with Order By)
Unions where branch data distribution affects global limit semantics
framework_notes: RBO rule in StarRocks Cascades framework. Matches LogicalLimit over LogicalUnion. Modifies the Union operator's inputs and sets limit property on the Union. Does not trigger physical enforcers directly as it is a logical transformation.

summary: Transforms Logical Apply operators representing EXISTS/NOT EXISTS subqueries into Left Outer Joins or Cross Joins with Aggregation, materializing the boolean result as a column while preserving left table cardinality.
ra_condition: Apply is existential, not semi/anti, no nested correlation subqueries
correctness_conditions: Subquery must be EXISTS or NOT EXISTS (isExistential)
Must not be represented as Semi/Anti Join (isUseSemiAnti is false)
Correlated predicates must contain at least one equality condition
No nested correlated subqueries allowed in the right input
Left table rows must be preserved (cardinality 1:N or 1:1)
performance_impact: Significantly improves performance by converting row-by-row Apply execution to set-based Join/Aggregate operations. Uncorrelated subqueries are optimized with Limit 1 and Count to avoid full scans.
edge_cases: Correlated subqueries with only non-equality predicates throw SemanticException
Nested correlated subqueries prevent rule application until inner ones are unnested
Semi/Anti join scenarios are handled by separate rules (ExistentialApply2SemiJoinRule)
Null handling in NOT EXISTS requires careful Left Outer Join null checking
framework_notes: RBO rule in Cascades framework matching LOGICAL_APPLY. Produces LogicalJoin, LogicalAggregate, LogicalProject. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion as it is a logical equivalence transformation.

summary: Transforms a logical COUNT(*) aggregation over a Hive/Iceberg/File scan into a SUM aggregation over a special metadata column, allowing the scan node to utilize row count statistics or metadata without reading full data blocks.
ra_condition: Aggregation is non-distinct COUNT, Scan is LOGICAL_HIVE_SCAN/ICEBERG_FILE_SCAN, session variable enabled
correctness_conditions: Aggregation must semantically represent COUNT(*) (row count), not COUNT(nullable_column)
Scan operator must correctly populate the ___count___ placeholder with row counts per split/partition
IFNULL wrapper is required to handle cases where sum returns NULL (e.g., empty input)
Grouping keys must be preserved and passed through the scan if present
performance_impact: Significant performance gain for COUNT queries on large external tables by avoiding data file reads if metadata is available; negligible overhead if optimization is not applicable at execution time
edge_cases: COUNT(DISTINCT ...) is not supported
COUNT on nullable columns may yield incorrect results if transformed blindly
Only supports Hive, Iceberg, and File scan operators
Controlled by session variable enable_rewrite_simple_agg_to_hdfs_scan
framework_notes: Cascades Transformation Rule (TF_REWRITE_SIMPLE_AGG); matches specific Memo group expressions containing LogicalAggr atop LogicalScan; modifies scan operator properties to enable count optimization flag

summary: Prunes unnecessary columns from logical scan operators (Hive, Iceberg, Hudi, etc.) by intersecting output columns with downstream required columns and predicate references, ensuring at least one materialized column is read for correctness.
ra_condition: B \subset A, B contains required and predicate columns, T is supported external table
correctness_conditions: All columns required by parent operators must be included in the new scan output
All columns referenced in the scan predicate must be included
At least one materialized column must be selected if the initial set contains only partition columns or is empty
Partition columns used must not be of type float, double, or decimal
performance_impact: Significantly reduces I/O and memory consumption for columnar storage formats by reading only necessary columns. Overhead is minimal during optimization.
edge_cases: SELECT COUNT(*) queries where no specific columns are required
Queries selecting only partition columns which may not be stored in data files
Tables partitioned by unsupported types (float/double/decimal) will throw an exception
Table Function Scans where column pruning logic might differ
framework_notes: Implements TransformerRule for LogicalScanOperators. Uses reflection to instantiate new scan operator type. Sets ScanOptimizeOption flags for count star optimization. Matches MultiOpPattern of supported external scan types.

summary: Transforms a logical scan on a system table with supported predicates into a logical values operator containing pre-evaluated rows, effectively inlining system metadata at compile time to avoid runtime scan overhead.
ra_condition: T is SystemTable AND p is supported for FE evaluation AND session rule enabled
correctness_conditions: The target table must be an instance of SystemTable
The predicate must be semantically safe to evaluate in the Frontend during optimization
The SystemTable implementation must support the evaluate method for the specific predicate
Column ordering must be preserved using getBaseSchema to match output references
performance_impact: Beneficial: Eliminates runtime scan overhead for system tables, reduces plan complexity, and pushes computation to compile time. Neutral: Minimal risk as system metadata is generally static during query compilation.
edge_cases: Predicates not supported by systemTable.supportFeEvaluation return empty list
Session variable isEnableEvaluateSchemaScanRule is disabled
SystemTable.evaluate throws NotImplementedException
Non-system tables passed to the rule are ignored
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_SCHEMA_SCAN pattern in Memo. Returns a new OptExpression with LogicalValuesOperator. Does not explicitly trigger enforcers but changes operator type which may affect downstream enforcement rules. Reduces memo exploration depth by replacing a scan branch with constant values.

summary: This rule rewrites a logical Iceberg scan into a union of clean data files and dirty data files anti-joined with equality delete files, ensuring deleted rows are filtered out according to Iceberg V2 specifications.
ra_condition: Table is Iceberg V2 format AND snapshot contains equality delete files
correctness_conditions: Iceberg table must be V2 format to support equality deletes
Equality delete files must be accessible for the specific snapshot ID
Equality field IDs in delete files must map correctly to table schema columns
Anti-join semantics must strictly exclude rows matching delete keys on equality columns
Partition evolution must be enabled via session variable if delete specs differ from data specs
performance_impact: Necessary for correctness when equality deletes exist; adds join and union overhead. Broadcast hint assumes delete files are small; large delete files may degrade performance due to join cost.
edge_cases: Tables with partition evolution require session variable enablement else throws exception
No equality deletes present (rule check returns false, no transformation)
Multiple distinct equality schemas increase anti-join chain depth linearly
Limit pushdown is applied only at the end of the anti-join chain potentially reducing early termination
framework_notes: RBO rule matching LogicalIcebergScanOperator group expressions. Prevents infinite recursion via setFromEqDeleteRewriteRule(true) flag. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion; expands single operator into complex tree of Unions and Left Anti Joins within the same group level initially.

summary: This rule transforms a single aggregation with a range filter into a Union of multiple aggregations, each covering a sub-range of the original filter, followed by a final aggregation. This enables fine-grained parallelism or partition pruning on range predicates.
ra_condition: Aggregation functions are mergeable (SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX), exactly one column has a lower+upper bound range predicate, and the range can be split into multiple sub-ranges.
correctness_conditions: Aggregation functions must be decomposable over UNION ALL (e.g., SUM, COUNT, MIN, MAX, HLL_UNION).
Distinct aggregations are not supported.
The union of split sub-ranges must semantically equal the original range predicate.
No having clause or predicate directly on the aggregation operator.
Plan must conform to simple SPJG (Select-Project-Join-GroupBy) structure.
performance_impact: Beneficial when underlying storage supports partition pruning per union branch or when parallel execution is improved by splitting large scans. Potentially harmful if range splitting overhead exceeds scan benefits or if data is not partitioned on the range column.
edge_cases: Distinct aggregation functions (e.g., COUNT DISTINCT).
Unsupported aggregation functions (e.g., complex UDFs).
Range predicates on multiple columns simultaneously.
Ranges that cannot be split into multiple sub-ranges (e.g., single point).
Aggregation operators with existing predicates (HAVING clause).
framework_notes: RBO rule in StarRocks Cascades optimizer. Matches LogicalAggr on top of LogicalFilter. Generates alternative LogicalUnion plans in the Memo. Does not explicitly insert Enforcers, relying on downstream rules for physical distribution.

summary: This rule pushes filter predicates down through a logical project operator. It rewrites predicates to reference the project's input columns instead of output aliases, splitting predicates into deterministic and non-deterministic sets to ensure correctness when the project contains non-deterministic expressions.
ra_condition: p' is the subset of p that is deterministic or does not depend on non-deterministic projected columns; p'' contains the remaining predicates.
correctness_conditions: Predicates referencing columns derived from non-deterministic functions in the project must remain above the project.
The rule is disabled if the project contains Lambda functions and the session variable EnableLambdaPushDown is false.
The rule is disabled if the project contains ASSERT_TRUE functions.
Column references in the predicate must be mappable to the project's input columns via the projection map.
performance_impact: Generally beneficial as it reduces the number of rows processed by the project and subsequent operators. Splitting predicates allows safe pushdown even when some expressions are non-deterministic, maximizing filtering early in the plan.
edge_cases: Queries selecting non-deterministic values (e.g., rand(), uuid()) aliased and filtered in the same query block.
Queries using Lambda functions in projections when lambda pushdown is disabled.
Predicates that simplify to constants (true/false) after being rewritten against project expressions.
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_FILTER on top of LOGICAL_PROJECT. Uses ReplaceColumnRefRewriter to map output columns to input columns. Applies scalar rewrite rules (FoldConstants, SimplifiedPredicate) to the pushed predicate. May generate a two-filter plan (one below project, one above) if non-deterministic predicates exist.

summary: This rule pushes filter predicates down through a Union operator, applying the filter to each individual branch of the union before the union operation occurs to reduce early cardinality.
ra_condition: Predicate p references columns available in all union branches (schema compatible)
correctness_conditions: Union inputs must have identical or compatible schemas
Predicate must be deterministic and side-effect free
Semantic equivalence of filter distribution over union holds
performance_impact: Beneficial when filters reduce cardinality significantly before union, reducing network shuffle and downstream processing; may duplicate predicate evaluation logic across branches.
edge_cases: Predicates referencing columns missing in some union branches
Union operations requiring distinct elimination (SELECT UNION vs UNION ALL)
Very small datasets where filter overhead exceeds data transfer savings
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_FILTER over LOGICAL_UNION in Memo; delegates logic to PushDownPredicateSetRule; generates new logical OptExpressions for exploration without immediate physical enforcement.

summary: Transforms a Logical Filter over a supported Logical Scan by merging the filter predicate into the scan predicate, simplifying it, and wrapping the result in an identity Logical Project.
ra_condition: R \in \text{SUPPORTED_SCAN_TYPES}
correctness_conditions: All columns referenced in predicate p must be available in scan R
Predicate evaluation must be safe to execute at the storage layer
Existing scan predicate q must be preserved and combined via AND
performance_impact: Significant performance gain by reducing data read from storage (predicate pushdown). Negligible cost from identity projection.
edge_cases: Scan types not in the SUPPORT set (e.g. custom scans)
Predicates that simplify to FALSE (resulting in empty scan)
Complex predicates that cannot be evaluated by the underlying storage engine
framework_notes: StarRocks Cascades RBO rule. Matches LOGICAL_FILTER parent and specific LOGICAL_SCAN children. Uses ScalarOperatorRewriter for normalization. Adds identity Project to maintain output schema structure in OptExpression. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion.

summary: This rule performs column pruning on Logical Union operators by identifying output columns required by the parent context and removing unreferenced columns from the Union schema and its children.
ra_condition: C ⊂ Output(∪) where C is the set of columns required by the parent operator
correctness_conditions: At least one column must remain in the output to preserve cardinality semantics (e.g., for COUNT(*))
Column mapping between Union output and child outputs must remain consistent by offset
Data types of corresponding columns across all union branches must remain compatible
performance_impact: Reduces memory consumption, I/O, and network shuffle size by eliminating unnecessary column processing in child operators and the Union itself; generally beneficial with negligible optimization overhead.
edge_cases: Queries requiring no specific columns (e.g., SELECT COUNT(*) FROM UNION) require keeping one dummy column
Child operators with reused column expressions where column_id differs from offset
Union branches with differing physical schemas but compatible logical types
framework_notes: Matches LOGICAL_UNION with PATTERN_MULTI_LEAF children in the Memo; updates RequiredColumns in TaskContext for child exploration; does not trigger physical Enforcer insertion as it is a logical transformation.

summary: This rule pushes a LogicalApply operator below a LogicalProject operator on its left input, transforming Apply(Project(Left), Right) into Project(Apply(Left, Right)).
ra_condition: ⋈ represents LogicalApply (uncorrelated scalar subquery); columns required by Apply exist in R
correctness_conditions: The Apply operator must represent an uncorrelated scalar subquery
The input to the Project (R) must contain all outer columns referenced by the Apply operator
The Project operator must not drop or alias columns required by the Apply predicate in a way that breaks references
The new Project must include the Apply output column in its projection map
performance_impact: Beneficial for enabling Project fusion (merging consecutive Project operators) and exposing the left child to further optimization rules. Generally neutral on execution cost as scalar subqueries preserve cardinality.
edge_cases: Queries where the Project renames or drops columns required by the Apply correlation
Correlated subqueries (check method filters these out)
Non-scalar subqueries (e.g., IN/EXISTS represented as Apply)
framework_notes: RBO rule in StarRocks Cascades framework. Matches LogicalApply with LogicalProject as left child. Does not trigger Enforcer insertion. Facilitates further logical transformations by simplifying the operator tree.

summary: This rule pushes a LogicalApply operator down through a LogicalProject operator on its right (subquery) input. It rewrites correlation column references to bypass the project and adds a new projection on top to maintain the original output schema.
ra_condition: Subquery is correlated; column references in Apply predicate/subquery can be rewritten through project mapping
correctness_conditions: The subquery must be correlated (contains outer references)
The LogicalProject must be a pure projection (no aggregation or cardinality change)
All column references in the Apply's subquery operator and predicate must be mappable to the Project's input columns
The new top-level Project must preserve the original Apply output schema (Left columns + Apply result)
performance_impact: Beneficial for exposing subquery internals to further optimization rules (e.g., predicate pushdown, join reordering) and enabling Apply-to-Join conversion. Minimal overhead from the added identity-like projection.
edge_cases: Non-correlated subqueries (rule check prevents firing)
Projects containing volatile functions or side effects
Complex column mappings that cannot be trivially rewritten
Cases where keeping the Project inside the subquery allows better local optimization
framework_notes: Cascades TransformationRule. Matches GroupExpressions where root is LogicalApply and right child is LogicalProject. Adds new GroupExpression to Memo without enforcing physical properties. Controls exploration by enabling subsequent rules on the exposed Right input.

summary: Optimizes simple MIN/MAX aggregations on partition columns by rewriting the query to scan only relevant partitions, use partition metadata values, or convert to a TopN operation, avoiding full table scans.
ra_condition: col is partition key, single partition column, no GROUP BY/HAVING/LIMIT/Predicate
correctness_conditions: Aggregated column must be the table's single partition column
Table must be a native OlapTable or Materialized View
Session variable isEnableRewritePartitionColumnMinMax must be true
No explicit partition or tablet hints in scan operator
For partition prune path: Table must be Duplicate Key model without deletions
Aggregation must contain only MIN/MAX functions without GROUP BY or HAVING
performance_impact: High benefit for large partitioned tables; reduces scan range to 1 or few partitions instead of full table; metadata lookup cost is negligible.
edge_cases: Multi-column partitioning (not supported)
Aggregate/Unique key tables (partition prune path disabled)
Tables with delete vectors (partition prune path disabled)
Queries with WHERE filters on base table
Queries with existing LIMIT clause
Empty partitions or null value partitions handling
framework_notes: StarRocks Cascades RBO rule; matches LogicalAggr->Project->OlapScan pattern; transforms Logical plan to Logical plan; returns empty list if no transformation applies.

summary: This rule inlines Common Table Expressions (CTEs) by removing the CTE anchor operator and directly using the CTE's producing expression when the optimizer context determines the CTE should be inlined.
ra_condition: context.getCteContext().needInline(CTE_Id) returns true
correctness_conditions: CTE must be safe to inline without changing query semantics
CTE should not have side effects that depend on evaluation order
Inlining must preserve identical query results
CTE reference count must allow inlining without excessive plan bloat
Non-recursive CTEs only (recursive CTEs cannot be inlined)
performance_impact: Beneficial when CTE is referenced once or few times, enabling further optimizations through the CTE body and reducing operator overhead. May hurt performance if CTE is referenced many times, causing expression duplication, increased optimization time, and larger plan memory footprint.
edge_cases: CTEs referenced multiple times may cause plan bloat from duplicated subexpressions
Recursive CTEs cannot be inlined and should be excluded
CTEs with side effects or non-deterministic functions should not be inlined
Large CTE bodies may significantly increase optimization time and memo size
CTEs used for materialization hints should not be inlined
framework_notes: This is an RBO logical equivalence rule in StarRocks Cascades framework. It matches LOGICAL_CTE_ANCHOR operators with two children (producer and consumer patterns). The rule does not trigger enforcer insertion as it's a pure logical transformation.
